coredata.dc:title: Tuning the stoichiometry of the pulsed laser deposited composite films
coredata.dc:creator: Mousavi, Seyedeh Soraya
Sajad, Batool
Simdar, Mehrnaz
coredata.dc:description: Graphene-based composite thin films were deposited on glass substrates using a laser-based approach, anointed Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD), at room temperature. To eliminate some technical fabricating hurdles, we studied the annealing temperature effects on the chemical properties of nanocomposite films and their photocatalytic activity. Results indicate that the stoichiometry of the composite films and their optical characteristics can be adjusted accurately through an optimized annealing temperature. We thoroughly investigated the characteristics of the layers using various analyses, x-ray diffraction analysis, scanning electron microscopy, energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy, and optical spectroscopies. We analyzed the film's functional groups and their alterations under different annealing temperatures using confocal Raman spectroscopy. Raman's results affirm the formation of a composite film with combined features of both precursors (ZnO and GQDs) at 300°C. We recorded a high degradation percentage of 84% for the sample to show a high photocatalytic ability to degrade dyes for optimized nanocomposite film. Under visible light irradiation, all ZnO-GQDs composite thin films showed a higher photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine 6 G than pristine ZnO. We found dye degradation to be of the first order, following the Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pulsed laser deposition
Nanocomposite
Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalyst
Thin film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and morphological analysis of the target
Structural and morphological analysis of the films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would also like to express their gratitude to the Photonics laboratory of Alzahra University for providing the possibility to complete this research. They gratefully acknowledge Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) for supporting this research under grant number 97021370.

The authors would also like to express their gratitude to the Photonics laboratory of Alzahra University for providing the possibility to complete this research. They gratefully acknowledge Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) for supporting this research under grant number 97021370.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM micrograph of (a) ZnO nanoparticles, (b) ZnO-GQDs nanocomposite, TEM images of (c) graphene quantum dots, and (d) ZnO-GQDs nanocomposite.

FESEM micrograph of (a-c) pure ZnO film annealed at 300 °C, (d-f) ZnO-GQDs nanocomposite film annealed at 150 °C, (g-i) ZnO-GQDs nanocomposite films annealed at 300 °C, and (j-l) ZnO-GQDs nanocomposite films annealed at 450 °C.
Raman spectra of prepared (a) Graphene quantum dots, and (b) ZnO nanoparticles, (c) Raman modes of ZnO.
A comparison between Raman spectra of the pure and composite films annealed at various annealing temperatures.
Normalized Raman spectra of the pure and composite films.
Elemental distributions of the pure and composite films annealed at various annealing temperatures.
A comparison between elemental mappings of the pure and composite films annealed at various annealing temperatures.
A comparison between crystal structures of the composite films annealed at various temperatures.
Photoluminescence spectra of the ZnO and ZnO-GQDs composite film annealed at 300 °C.
schematics of charge carrier transitions between bands (band to band and excitonic emissions) for a composite film, and its photocatalytic mechanism.
Photodegradation of Rhodamine 6 G after 60 min using ZnO and ZnO-GQDs composite films annealed at various annealing temperatures.

(a) Photodegradation rates of Rhodamine 6 G Vs. time using ZnO and ZnO-GQDs composite film annealed at various annealing temperatures, (b) kinetic rate constant (kr) of the films as a function of time.
The photocatalytic features of the prepared films.

head.ce:title: Tuning the stoichiometry of the pulsed laser deposited composite films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Faculty of Physics, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Faculty of Physics, Alzahra University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Faculty of Physics, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene-based composite thin films were deposited on glass substrates using a laser-based approach, anointed Pulsed Laser Deposition (PLD), at room temperature. To eliminate some technical fabricating hurdles, we studied the annealing temperature effects on the chemical properties of nanocomposite films and their photocatalytic activity. Results indicate that the stoichiometry of the composite films and their optical characteristics can be adjusted accurately through an optimized annealing temperature. We thoroughly investigated the characteristics of the layers using various analyses, x-ray diffraction analysis, scanning electron microscopy, energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy, and optical spectroscopies. We analyzed the film's functional groups and their alterations under different annealing temperatures using confocal Raman spectroscopy. Raman's results affirm the formation of a composite film with combined features of both precursors (ZnO and GQDs) at 300°C. We recorded a high degradation percentage of 84% for the sample to show a high photocatalytic ability to degrade dyes for optimized nanocomposite film. Under visible light irradiation, all ZnO-GQDs composite thin films showed a higher photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine 6 G than pristine ZnO. We found dye degradation to be of the first order, following the Langmuir-Hinshelwood mechanism.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Functional composite materials are the main parts of daily life, such as coatings for decoration, sensors, or medical applications. For example, magnetic, optical, and electrical coatings can be utilized for manufacturing modern devices [1]. There are various approaches to the synthesis of nanostructures. These synthesis methods include solution-based and vapor-phase routes. The solution-based procedure is the simplest with the least energy-consuming. Through this method, one can control the size and morphology of the nanostructures [2,3]. Thermal evaporation, pulsed laser deposition [4], physical vapor deposition, chemical vapor deposition, metal-organic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD), plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD), and molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) are examples of the vapor phase methods [5].

Pulsed laser deposition is one of the reliable techniques for meticulously preparing these films. In the same way that pulsed laser ablation (PLA) uses lasers to ablate a surface, pulsed laser deposition (PLD) uses lasers to produce a high-temperature plasma (T > 104 K) for depositing materials on a substrate [6]. With the ease of obtaining multi-component and uniform film (of the desired stoichiometric ratio), a high deposition rate, short test period, and low substrate temperature requirements are the main advantages of PLD [7,8]. Its operational flexibility and simplicity, high development potential, no limits on the type of PLD targets, and arbitrarily adjustable parameters naturally make it an attractive method [6]. The capability of exploiting multi-target components makes fabricating multilayered films and heterojunctions possible. Moreover, creating functional composite films with tunable stoichiometry is also facilitated using composite targets. Obtaining a composite film with the desired properties using the deposition techniques requires precise process controlling [4].

Besides their wide range of applications, Zinc oxide (ZnO)-based composites are gaining much attention among nanocomposite materials due to their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and affordability. Despite the large number of publications that have discussed the various aspects and applications of ZnO, we are still witnessing the further utilization of this substance combined with other materials in today's modern applications [9–11]. Zinc oxide with various morphologies is utilized in solar cells, photocatalysis, batteries [12,13], and chemical sensors. Low toxicity and strong ultraviolet absorption make it an excellent candidate for biological applications. The strong and stiff structure of ZnO NPs makes them useful in the ceramic industry. Biological sensing, biological labeling, gene delivery, drug delivery [14], and nanomedicine are the other applications of zinc oxide nanostructures [15]. Photocatalytic applications of ZnO are both scientifically and industrially significant [16]. Photocatalytic technology represents an eco-friendly and sustainable route to solve environmental and energy issues. Producing an applicable photocatalyst involves various criteria; Light absorption ability, the density of active sites, redox capacity, and photo-induced electron-hole recombination rate are the main factors. Intrinsic semiconductor photocatalysts cannot meet these requirements. So, their capabilities have to be modified [17]. Combining ZnO with carbon-based components creates a composite with promoted characteristics suitable for its utilization. These composites also offer unique features in energy storage and photocatalytic uses [18]. Most notably, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have led to increased advancements over the past decade. In essence, GQDs are fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) consisting of layers of graphene (whether they are single or multiple) regardless of their size. GQDs have several advantages over graphene, such as their higher solubility in water, a tunable bandgap, and ease of surface functionalization, which is affected by edges and quantum confinement effects [19]. Additional benefits include their surface topography, which has multiple functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and epoxide) that act as active sites on the edges or basal planes. They offer unparalleled benefits for designing biosensing platforms [20]. GQDs have displayed significant momentum in numerous fields, such as energy devices, catalysis, sensing, photodynamic and photothermal therapy, drug delivery, and bioimaging [21]. In addition to their excellent biocompatibility, GQDs can interact with various biomolecules and other materials via π–π, electrostatic, and covalent interactions. Moreover, GQDs provide inherent photoluminescence (PL) and simultaneously can regulate their properties during or after synthesis, making them the most prominent candidate in the carbon family. GQDs are known for their PL properties extremely useful when fabricating optical biosensors for tracking numerous analytes within their matrices [22]. GQDs synthetic routes can be categorized into two types; top-down and bottom-up. Hydro- and solvothermal synthesis, electrochemical exfoliation, and liquid-phase exfoliation are the techniques employed as top-down methods. Bottom-up routes involve the assembly of GQDs from small precursor molecules. Pyrolysis of precursors and microwave-assisted synthesis are classified as bottom-up methods [23]. Most of the novel technologies have to utilize composites in their film form. However, achieving films with the desired composite's chemical structure is challenging. The expansion velocities and angular distributions of light elements in a plume differ from those of heavier elements in PLD [24,25]. The species distribution of a multi-elemental plume will depend on the respective mass ratios of the target species, as well as the pressure at which the deposition takes place [26]. As for the deposition conditions, an appropriate target composition or background gas is necessary to achieve the desired film composition. The study offers two practical approaches for obtaining ZnO-GQDs films with the desirable components. First, prepare a target using the chemically synthesized nanocomposite. Then, control the film's chemical bondings by tuning the post-annealing temperature.

As described in the reference, ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized by the sol-gel route [27]. In this regard, Zn (CH3COO)2·2 H2O (99.5% Merck), Triethylamine (TEA) (99% Merck), and absolute ethanol (99% Merck) were used as the precursors. Zinc acetate dihydrate was dissolved in ethanol and TEA (with a molar ratio of 2:5 TEA to zinc acetate dihydrate). After ∼30 min, the solution became colorless, homogeneous, and transparent. The white ZnO precipitates dried at 120 °C for an hour in an oven to prepare the powder. Then, it was annealed in a furnace at 400 °C for two hours.

Direct pyrolysis of citric acid yielded GQDs, as described by Dong et al. [28]. 2 g citric acid (99%, Sigma Aldrich) is placed in a beaker and heated to 200 °C for 5 min. The obtained orange liquid (after 30 min) is neutralized to pH 7.0 by adding NaOH solution under vigorous stirring. Then, with a 6–8 kg Dalton tube, the neutralized liquid was dialyzed for 24 h.

For the preparation of the composite, 0.1 g ZnO powder and 5 mL GQDs were mixed through stirring for two hours. Then, the mixture was dried in an oven at 50 °C overnight. At last, the obtained powder was annealed at 150 °C for an hour [1].

A launched PLD setup consists of a high vacuum-mixing table, focusing optics, and a Q-switch Nd: YAG (neodymium-doped yttrium aluminum garnet) laser. It offers a 1064 nm wavelength and 5 ns pulse duration. At an optimized pulse repetition rate (3.3 Hz), parallel target, and substrate holder (with 3.5 cm distance), the laser beam irradiates at a 45° angle with an energy of 163 mJ on the target.

The target was composed using the synthesized nanocomposite powder through a hydrodynamically pressing process (at room temperature). The substrates were ultrasonically and chemically cleaned in soapsuds, acetone, and ethanol for 10 min and dried in the nitrogen flow.

The chamber was evacuated up to 10–5 mbar pressure by a diffusion pump and a one-stage rotary backup pump. The deposition was performed without any gas insertion and heating modes. The samples were irradiated (by 12,000 laser pulses) to achieve the same thickness. They were post-annealed at three different temperatures of 150°C, 300°C, and 450°C for two hours. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Raman spectroscopy characterized the films' surface morphology and chemical structure. Energy Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDS / EDAX) also quantitatively measured elemental composition and gave insight into the elements' distribution. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra also determined the films' optical properties and defects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological analysis of the target
ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the changes that occurred during the deposition and annealing process, we first studied the morphology of the nanocomposite and its components through their electron microscopic micrographs. Fig. 1 presents images obtained using transmission electron microscopy and scanning electron microscopy of ZnO nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots, and ZnO-GQDs nanocomposites.

Fig. 1(a and b) show typical SEM images of ZnO and ZnO-GQDs composites samples prepared through the chemical procedure. A TEM image with high magnification (Fig. 1c) displays that the distribution size of GQDs is in the 1–5 nm range. High-magnification TEM images (Fig. 1d) reveal graphene dots surrounding ZnO nanoparticles.
Compounding ZnO nanoparticles with graphene quantum dots enhanced their dimensions, as seen in Fig. 1. To determine the phase purity of ZnO, we performed an XRD analysis (supplementary 1).
It is apparent from the sharpness of the diffraction peaks that the sample as prepared has a high crystalline quality due to the hexagonal wurtzite structure (JCPDS 36–1451).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological analysis of the films
ce:section.ce:para: FESEM (Field emission scanning electron microscopy) images of pure ZnO and composite films prepared at the same deposition conditions, but annealed at different temperatures, are shown in Fig. 2. For better understanding, pure ZnO was merely annealed at 300°C (the optimized annealing temperature of the composite films). Fig. 2a–c shows the SEM images of the pure ZnO films annealed at 300°C.

The composite film annealed at 150°C indicated a dense and smooth sheet structure (Fig. 2d–f). The average grain size of this sample ranges from 20 to 30 nanometers. Regardless, several colony structures appeared on its surface after annealing. SEM images of the composite films after 300 °C annealing are illustrated in Fig. 2g–i.

The surface morphology of the annealed films thoroughly changes at 300°C compared to the sample annealed at 150°C. The surface structure of the layers undergoes considerable change. Comparing morphologies of the pure and composite films annealed at the same temperature indicates few grain sizes and morphology differences. Temperature increases up to 450°C lead to an enhancement in grain sizes. Because a high annealing temperature can effectively improve the atomic mobility of the layers, thereby allowing the atoms to be located in their energy-matching position and raising the stability of the films [29].
Cross-section micrographs were used to determine the thickness of deposited thin films; measured values range from 950 to 1100 nm (see supplementary).

In Raman scattering measurements, photons interact with electrons in the lattice and thus provide information about both vibrational and electronic structures. One can measure phonons involved in the scattering by considering the fundamental Raman selection rule. However, only phonons near Γ (wave vector q≈0) are measured.

The advanced Raman spectroscopy used a laser with a wavelength of 532 nm. The presence of characteristic graphene peaks (D and G) in the synthesized samples confirms the formation of GQDs. As Fig. 3(a), the Raman spectrum is resolved into two distinctive D and G bands at 1338.37 and 1576.04 cm−1, respectively.

Observation of the D band is due to breathing vibration modes in the sp3 carbon atoms in the plane, which come from TO phonons around the Brillouin zone's K-point. On the other hand, the G-band was attributed to the vibration of sp2 carbon atoms and corresponded to the E2g phonon at the Brillouin zone center.
The intensity of the G peak is proportional to the number of sp2 bonds in the region excited [30,31]. Furthermore, a peak at 1420 cm−1 (labeled D3) relates to CO/C-O edge functional groups [30].

The D band intensity is very sensitive to the graphene edges and is high for armchair edges and low for zigzag edges. According to the literature, GQDs appear as circular shapes with zigzag edges for these sizes and water-based grown graphene quantum dots (smaller than 5 nm), as confirmed based on TEM images [32].

The intensity ratio of D to G peaks (ID / IG) oxidation rate and size of sp2 ring clusters (in the sp2/sp3 hybrid lattice). A graphene lattice with a ratio exceeding one is distorted and severely defective. But if this ratio is less than 1, it denotes the high quality of the lattice [33].

According to the Fig. 3, the G band with greater peak intensity than the D band (ratio of 0.6) indicates the high-quality prepared GQDs. Fig. 3(b) illustrates the Raman spectrum of the ZnO nanoparticles. According to XRD results, synthesized ZnO and ZnO-GQDs possessed a wurtzite crystal structure, which belongs to the space group P63mc (See Supplementary). This arrangement gives rise to polar symmetry along the hexagonal vertical axis (c axis). The wurtzite structure of ZnO crystal also has C6v symmetry.

There are four atoms in the hexagonal unit cell leading to 12 phonon branches, nine optical, and three acoustics, classified according to the following irreducible representations:
Γopt=A1+2E1+2E2+B1

Where A and B modes have one-fold degeneracy and E modes are two-fold. Acoustic phonons are comprised of an A1 mode and an E1 pair. There are nine optical phonons in total, with one A1 branch (Raman and IR active), one doubly degenerate E1 branch (Raman and IR active), and one doubly degenerates E2 branch (Raman active only), and two inactive B1 branches. B1 modes are silent. E1 and A1 are polar modes that are both Raman and infrared active. E2 modes (E2 low and E2 high) are nonpolar and Raman active only. In both E2 modes, the atoms move perpendicular to the c-axis, and the neighbor ions move oppositely (Fig. 3(b)).

Therefore, summing up the displacements of the ions equals zero, resulting in no net-induced polarization. For this reason, these two modes identify as nonpolar modes [34]. A1 and E1 modes are polar branches with longitudinal optical (LO) and transversal optical (TO) components. The A1, E1, and E2 modes are first-order Raman-active modes.

ZnO phonon mode peaks are located at 100, 385, 439, and 585 cm−1 indicating the E2 low, A1-TO, E2 high, and A1-LO/E1-LO, respectively. But here, the peak located near 100 cm−1 shows a negligible intensity. At about 200 cm−1, a second-order phonon mode can be assigned to 2E2 low.

The multi-phonon scattering modes with peaks at 329, 579, 664, and 1065 cm−1 are related to the E2 high-E2 low, E1-TO+E2 low, 2(E2 high-E2 low), and A1-TO+E1-TO+E2 low, respectively. Moreover, the acoustic combination of A1 and E2 is observable near 1100 cm−1. The E2 high Raman mode observed at 439 cm−1 can be assigned to the oxygen vibration [31,34,35]. Overall, the spectrum is therefore consistent with a wurtzite-structured ZnO lattice.

Here are remarkable results deduced by comparing pure ZnO and composite films annealed at various temperatures. Producing composite films containing both ingredient peaks with the best crystal quality is of utmost importance.

Fig. 4 represents all the spectra in a frame. Accordingly, the Raman spectrum of pure ZnO film is approximately the same as its nanoparticles. Despite this, composite films include some additional peaks that indicate GQDs exist and form bonds between ZnO and functional groups of GQDs.

But they possess thoroughly peculiar chemical bonds that brought them different facilities. Besides the varied peak intensity, the spectra of the composite films experienced some Raman shifts. Accordingly, the peak at 100 cm−1, dominated by the vibrations of the heavy Zn sublattice, appears as the sample annealed at 450 °C. A higher annealing temperature reconstructs the lattice structure [8].

At about 250 cm−1, the 2E2 low second-order phonon modes are distinguishable for all samples. Four Raman peaks can be detected in the spectrum of pure ZnO film at 330, 437, 526, and 564 cm−1, respectively. ( Fig. 5).

The peak at 330 cm−1 relates to the second-order Raman scattering of E2 high-E2 low. This second-order Raman scattering is appeared due to the impurities, disorders, and defects in the entire crystal. The peak emerged with a slight shift and different intensities for the other samples. It has the lowest intensity for the composite film annealed at 300 °C, as affirmed by PL and XRD results.
Another intense peak at 437 cm−1 appears in all films, attributed to E2 high mode, E2 high mode is typical of P63mc symmetry and originates from the oxygen vibration in ZnO.

According to the publications, the hybrid of graphene-based materials with ZnO reduced disorder concentration and decreased the intensity of E2 high, as occurred in samples annealed at 150 and 300 °C. However, a band located at 530 and 545 cm−1 appeared with a higher intensity for these films. It attributes to the higher amounts of GQDs in these samples that exhibited surface optical phonon. The intensity of this mode is significantly higher for these two samples.

The spectrum of ZnO-GQDs.450 also displays this peak but with a lower magnitude than its E2 high peak and a noticeable Raman shift to the lower energies. In addition, a broad signal appears between 500 cm−1 and 600 cm−1, peaking at about 530 cm−1 to 580 cm−1. Generally, these Raman shift peaks at frequencies 574–579 cm−1 and 583–591 cm−1 correspond to A1-LO and E1-LO modes, respectively. There have been several proposed assignments and activation mechanisms for A1-LO/E1-LO phonon, but two of them are the most probable:
First, the mode induced by defects, such as oxygen vacancies and zinc interstitials;.

Second, a quasimode of mixed A1 and E1 symmetry due to crystallite-orientation effects [31]. The mode involves LO phonons, which, due to their long-range electrostatic field, can couple with electrons and modify the optoelectronic characteristics of ZnO-based materials [30]. Moreover, due to impurity effects, each sample experienced a peak shift for these modes towards lower wavenumber. The reinforcement should result from a combination of resonance at the excitation wavelength and impurity-induced scattering [30]. These modes are significantly higher for the composite samples, especially ZnO-GQDs. 150, and ZnO-GQDs.300. In conclusion, these modes appeared due to the higher residue of GQDs. For ZnO-GQDs.450, these peaks have much lower intensities. The peak locates near 1100 cm−1 is mostly detectable for pure-ZnO and ZnO-GQDs.450 and slight for ZnO-GQDs. 150 and ZnO-GQDs. 300. In the region between 1200 and 1700 cm−1, three intensive peaks near 1330 cm−1, 1420 cm−1, and 1570 cm−1 are thoroughly detectable for the composite films. These peaks are the same as the GQDs spectrum. They are dominant in the spectrum of the sample annealed at 300 °C. Their intensity is significantly lower for ZnO-GQDs.450. It means that high annealing temperatures reconstruct the structure of the ZnO with GQDs. According to the high percentage of carbon recorded in the EDS analysis, oxygen vacancies have been replaced by carbon in this sample.

Fig. 6 represents the atomic percentages of the film's constitutions as bar charts. EDS analysis determined the film's elemental compositions. Comparatively, the mass fraction and atomic number percentage of carbon are near zero for ZnO, as expected. However, its variation for the composite films is thoroughly different. This result indicates that the carbon and oxygen percentages are almost equal for the annealed film at 450 °C .
At higher temperatures, carbon and oxygen contents of GQDs have been diffused in the crystal structure of ZnO, and its lattice structure reconstructs again.

The samples annealed at 300 °C, pure ZnO and ZnO-GQDs.300, contain the highest zinc content (zinc-rich), as the XRD spectra confirmed. The high annealing temperature results in lower contents of GQDs on the surface of this film compared to the sample annealed at 150 °C. However, ZnO-GQDs.150 indicates an oxygen-rich composition with a high percentage of carbon. It can occur due to the higher content of GQDs surrounding ZnO, and its functional groups contain OH bonds. Fig. 7 depicts the elemental mapping of the composite films.

While we expected the sample annealed at 450 °C to have a lower content of carbon and a more organized crystal structure, both EDS and XRD results confirm this sample's high carbon content and deformed ZnO crystallinity.

Comparing the XRD patterns of the composite films results in some points: XRD patterns of all the samples contain a peak near 2θ= 25°, attributed to the GQDs. The intensity of this peak is much higher for ZnO-GQDs. 450.

However, its full-width half maximum (FWHM) is much lower for ZnO-GQDs. 150. The peak intensity of GQDs is mostly higher than ZnO peaks for ZnO-GQDs.150 and ZnO-GQDs.450. The XRD pattern of the sample annealed at 300°C contains both crystalline peaks with a relatively appropriate resolution. We observed that the peaks became sharper and more well-defined for ZnO-GQDs.300. These results agree with Raman and EDS results. According to Fig. 8, a significant shift (δ˚) is observable for all three peaks of ZnO-GQDs.150, ZnO-GQDs.450 compared to ZnO standard pattern (JCPDS 36–1451). A peak shift detected for (100) from the normal (2θ=31.770) attributed to lattice defects. The first peaks were recorded at 32.18, 31.78, and 31.82 for ZnO-GQDs.150, ZnO-GQDs.300, and ZnO-GQDs.450, respectively. We can also see that the peaks (200), (112), and (201) of ZnO-GQDs.300 have evolved to be clearer and more well-defined when compared to the other samples. The mean grain sizes (D) of each film have been estimated from the FWHM (β) of the preferred peak in the XRD spectra, using Scherer’s formula [36]:
D =
k λ
β cos
θ
Where k is constant, λ is Cu Kα radiation wavelength, β is full width half maximum. The average crystallite size calculated for ZnO-GQDs.150, ZnO-GQDs.300, and ZnO-GQDs.450 were 23.3, 36.2, and 42.9 nm, respectively.
Further, a photoluminescence spectroscopy was conducted to estimate the defects of ZnO and ZnO-GQD.300.

Fig. 9 demonstrates the luminescence spectrum of the samples at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Room temperature photoluminescence of the films reveals broad UV and visible emissions. The first peak links to the band–band mechanisms, and the latter (excitonic emission) relates to defects in ZnO. Generally, the whole spectrum of the composite experienced a reduction in intensity compared to pure ZnO. PL spectrum can directly reflect the separation situation of photo-induced charge carriers. Generally, the lower the PL intensity, the lower the recombination rate of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, and the higher the photocatalytic activity of semiconductor photocatalysts [37]. A remarkable reduction in the excitonic peak intensity of ZnO-GQDs. 300 is evidence of its lower density of defects. The excitonic PL spectrum reveals some essential information about surface defects, oxygen vacancies, and surface states, which can strongly affect photocatalytic reactions ( Fig. 10).

The defects associated with ZnO within the band gap are either donor-type zinc interstitials (Zn++ i, Zn+ i, Zn*i) and oxygen vacancies (VO ++, VO +, and VO) or acceptor-type zinc vacancies (VZn″ and VZn′) [38]. It primarily related the blue region at the end of the PL spectrum to Zn defects. The multi-peak bands of the films between 400 and 480 nm were ascribed to the recombination of the electrons from the shallow-level donor of zinc interstitials (Zni) and a series of extended Zni states to holes in the valence band [39].

O-vacancies in ZnO are well-known for enhancing photocatalytic activity despite other intrinsic crystal defects. Oxygen vacancies with lots of localized electrons enhance the adsorption of pollutant molecules. The surface oxygen vacancies can capture electrons or holes to reduce charge carrier recombination and promote the transfer of charge carriers to adsorbed pollutants [40]. Defect emission bands in the visible range – a blue-green band (λbg ∼ 500–560 nm) attributed to the oxygen vacancies of ZnO films. VO + state captures an electron from the conduction band and forms a neutral state (VO *), from which a transition to the valence band occurs by a green emission at the 500 nm wavelength [39,41].

The emission at the wavelength of 538 nm was generated by surface trapping of photo-generated holes, followed by recombination with an electron 1 + charged oxygen vacancy (VO +). [38]. This peak thoroughly disappeared for the composite one. It means that oxygen functional groups linked to GQDs reformed the surface defects of ZnO.

Both Zni and VO capture charge carriers, delaying the recombination process. The results suggest that Zni and related defects are less efficient than VO in the photocatalytic process. It is mainly because of the accessibility of these defects for photocatalytic activity [38,41].

During the PL process, oxygen vacancies and zinc defects can bind photo-induced electrons to form excitons so that the PL signal can occur, and the larger the content of oxygen vacancy or defect, the stronger the PL signal [37,38]. However, in the composite sample, the reduction of the oxygen defects and the presence of GQDs with their oxygen-based functional groups significantly enhanced the photocatalytic ability of the nanocomposite film. Many publications confirmed graphene quantum dots (GQD) are promising visible-light photocatalysts for organic dye degradation [42–44]. The nature of functional groups present in the synthesized GQD was determined by employing FTIR (see supplementary).

Fig. 11 illustrates the photocatalytic ability of the films in the degradation of Rhodamine 6 G (Rh). The concentration of Rh was constantly adjusted to 10 ppm in the water. For this aim, UV–Vis absorption spectra of the Rh degradation versus time in the presence of ZnO and composite films were recorded. The experiments were performed under white light illumination of (8 watts) and by applying four pieces of each film with a total area of 2 × 2 cm2.

Fig. 11 compares the photocatalytic ability of pure ZnO and composite films for specific times (about 60 min). According to the results, inserting GQDs enhances the photocatalytic activity of the ZnO remarkably. The photocatalytic ability of composite films is mainly improved due to the formation of a Z-type heterojunction system (between ZnO and GQDs bands). Meanwhile, the electrons and holes can shift between their conduction and valence bands, reducing the electron-hole recombination rate.

Considering the relevant conduction band position of ZnO and GQDs [27], the photo-induced electrons can readily transfer between them, thus increasing the separation rate of charge carriers and decreasing the excitonic PL intensity [37]. The results demonstrate that the composite film's photocatalytic activity increases as the excitonic PL spectrum weakens. The weaker the excitonic PL spectrum, the higher the photocatalytic activity.

The photocatalytic degradation as a function of time can be calculated using the equation described as follows [44]:
Degradation %=(C0-Ct/C0) ×100

All the samples showed higher photocatalytic activity compared to pure ZnO. However, ZnO-GQDs.450 film degraded Rhodamine slightly more than ZnO (about 7% higher). A photodegradation rate of 84% for ZnO-GQDs.300 states that the sample exploits the benefits of both ZnO and GQDs together. Its photocatalytic capability was enhanced by the higher density of carboxyl groups and other oxygen species, an improved ZnO crystal structure, and lower excitonic emissions. ZnO-GQDs.150 exhibited an appropriate photodegradation activity of 59%, ranked second among the others.

The constant reaction rate kr (the kinetics of the photocatalytic reactions for Rh) calculated by the pseudo-first-order Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics, accordingly:
Ln(Ct/C0) = -krt

A high kr implies further photodegradation by photocatalysis of the dye's molecular structure [44]. The calculated kinetic rate constants (kr) as a function of time are plotted in Fig. 12, and listed in Table1. The lowest and highest kr values were recorded for pure ZnO and ZnO-GQDs. 300, 0.01 min−1 and 0.05 min−1, respectively. These results imply an increase in the photocatalytic activity of ZnO film by the addition of GQDs and optimizing annealing temperature.

Reusability is an essential parameter for utilizing a photocatalyst. In this way, we tested the recyclability of composite films in four cycles of dye degradation. The results indicate no significant decrease in degradation efficiency across the four runs.

In this paper, composite films were deposited through the pulsed laser deposition method. Films were prepared in the same conditions but annealed at various temperatures. We analyzed the film's structural, optical, and photocatalytic properties as a function of the annealing temperature. Chemical compounds in the composite films were analyzed using confocal Raman spectroscopy. A Langmuir–Hinshelwood type kinetic model proved suitable to describe the experimental data. According to the Raman spectra, the films annealed at 300°C contain both peaks of ZnO and GQDs with the highest intensity. Based on the results, the ZnO-GQDs.300 possesses the best crystal structure, stoichiometry, and optical properties. The PL spectra affirm the reduced density of the defects compared to pure ZnO film. A photodegradation rate of 84% for ZnO-GQDs.300 states that the sample exploits the benefits of both ZnO and GQDs together. It occurred due to the higher crystallinity of ZnO-GQDs.300. Moreover, according to the PL results, the reduced recombination rate by improving the long pathway for carrier transport enhances the carrier lifetime. The long carrier lifetime is required to hold carriers at the material surface for reacting with surrounding contaminants. The enhanced photocatalysis mechanism of composite films attributes to their Z-type heterojunction system. Meanwhile, the electrons and holes can shift between their conduction and valence bands, reducing the electron-hole recombination rate.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would also like to express their gratitude to the Photonics laboratory of Alzahra University for providing the possibility to complete this research. They gratefully acknowledge Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) for supporting this research under grant number 97021370.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.171102.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
